text: The surgeon retracts the heavy colon upwards towards the anterior abdominal wall to locate the inferior mesenteric pedicle. The dissection proceeds distally to identify the mesorectum before moving proximally.

The surgeon uses a lighted stent to guide the dissection around the left ureter, which is affected by inflammation. Visible inflammation surrounds the pedicle, and the assistant uses suction to aid in finding anatomical planes. Dissection is performed with scissors and minimal electrocautery, while insufflation helps separate tissue planes.

The surgeon continues dissection around the superior hemorrhoidal vein and artery, carefully removing surrounding fat to separate these vessels individually. The area shows significant inflammation, and extreme care is taken due to the proximity of the left ureter to the vessels. The lighted stents are not visible in the background, indicating they have been lifted, necessitating careful identification of the ureter before proceeding with pedicle dissection.

The surgeon selects a point at the proximal rectum and distal sigmoid colon, using a vessel sealer to clear the mesorectum for improved hemostasis. The mesorectum is circumferentially cleaned to prevent bleeding during anastomosis.

The surgeon cleans the distal colon and takes the mesentery at the proximal point of the descending colon to prepare for the colorectal anastomosis. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess perfusion, showing excellent blood flow both distally and proximally. The rectum is straightened, and dilators are introduced to determine the appropriate size for the EA stapler.

The surgeon decides to use a 29 EA stapler for the anastomosis. A colotomy is made to pass the anvil, which is then connected to the stapler spike through the twelfth port. The proximal rectum is stapled, but the tissue is initially too thick for the green load stapler to fire. Additional cleaning of lateral attachments is performed to facilitate stapler firing.

The surgeon attaches the spike of the anvil to the anvil itself, avoiding placing sutures at the tip to facilitate extraction. A colotomy is made, and the spike is passed more proximally. The distal portion of the colon is stapled off, creating an end-to-side anastomosis between the end of the rectum and the side of the descending colon. The surgeon carefully maneuvers the spike, ensuring it does not puncture the tinea, and retrieves it completely before stapling the distal colon.
Following the creation of the anastomosis, the surgeon observes a small portion of the descending colon with an exposed staple line and places a couple of bicoral sutures at the site.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer as a needle holder to suture comfortably. A flexible endoscopy is performed to inspect the lumen and conduct an air leak test, ensuring the staple line is intact and there is no bleeding. Despite the large specimen size, a small Pfannenstiel incision is made to extract the specimen.
